retrieved_docs.title: Extrapyramidal Side Effects -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Acute dystonic reactions are typically transient, but in late-onset and persistent tardive dystonia, symptoms can persist for years. [58] A study involving 107 cases of tardive dystonia reported that only 14% of patients achieved remission over a mean follow-up period of 8.5 years. [59] Acute akathisia may spontaneously resolve or improve with appropriate medication; however, there are reported cases of tardive akathisia persisting over many years. [60] Tardive dyskinesia may be unable to be reversed, with a cumulative persistence rate as high as 82% in a study involving patients with schizophrenia. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Extrapyramidal Side Effects -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Acute laryngeal dystonia caused by dopamine receptor-blocking medications can result in life-threatening airway obstruction and requires emergency treatment. Clozapine causes fewer extrapyramidal symptoms compared to other antipsychotic medications and is preferred in patients with psychosis and parkinsonian symptoms. [66] Approving VMAT2 inhibitors to treat patients with tardive dyskinesia is a significant advance. [67] Antipsychotic medications administered at doses higher than the minimum dosage that causes extrapyramidal symptoms are likely to increase adverse effects without additional therapeutic benefit. Although anticholinergic medications can be effective for medication-induced parkinsonism and dystonia, they are not recommended for tardive dyskinesia, akathisia, or neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Anticholinergic medications have a high adverse effect burden and should be prescribed for appropriate reasons at the lowest effective dose for only a limited period. [68]

retrieved_docs.title: Delusional Misidentification Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: As schizophrenia is highly associated with DMSs, patients may have significantly lower life expectancy. A meta-analysis over multiple continents showed an associated average of 14.5 years of potential life with higher rates for me. Potential life lost was the least amongst patients in Asia and most significant in Africa. [51] In patients with an identifiable lesion, a delay (median 60 days) between injury and the delusion was often noted, with a median duration of 42 days before resolution. Medical or psychiatric decompensation leads to reoccurrence and longer durations. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizophrenia -- Treatment / Management -- Antipsychotic Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotic medications are divided into first-generation antipsychotics (FGAs) and second-generation antipsychotics (SGAs). While SGAs typically have fewer extrapyramidal side effects and cause less tardive dyskinesia than FGAs, most SGAs, like olanzapine, quetiapine, clozapine, and risperidone, carry a higher risk of metabolic syndrome, especially olanzapine and clozapine. [23] [24] Despite these differences, no clear preference exists between SGAs and FGAs overall, and individual efficacy varies, with clozapine potentially more effective in treatment-resistant schizophrenia and for those patients with a high risk for aggression or suicide. [21] The selection of specific treatments considers factors such as the severity of the illness, patient history of treatment response, the balance between medication efficacy and side effects, and patient preferences. Both FGA and SGA long-acting injectable (LAI) antipsychotic medications can be used to address treatment nonadherence or based on patient preference. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Extrapyramidal Side Effects -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Abouzaid and colleagues recently evaluated the economic burden of extrapyramidal symptoms due to atypical antipsychotics in patients with schizophrenia. During a 12-month follow-up period, 12.6% of patients experienced extrapyramidal symptoms. Compared to patients without extrapyramidal symptoms, those who did experience extrapyramidal symptoms had more schizophrenia-related and all-cause hospitalizations, schizophrenia-related emergency room visits, and higher schizophrenia-related and all-cause total healthcare, inpatient, and prescription medication costs. [69]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: For most patients in whom the offending drug is discontinued, the prognosis is good. An interprofessional approach to the education of the family, monitoring, and treatment by clinicians, nurses, and pharmacists will provide the best outcomes. [Levell 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Early mortality reports were greater than 30% for NMS; however, increased awareness, earlier detection, and better supportive care have reduced mortality to less than 10%. With early recognition and aggressive treatment, most patients will fully recover in 2 to 14 days. Delayed treatment may result in significant morbidity such as residual catatonia, parkinsonism, renal, or cardiopulmonary complications. When fatal, deaths are usually due to cardiac arrhythmias, disseminated intravascular coagulation, respiratory failure, or renal failure. Many patients may be successfully restarted on neuroleptic medications, but some will develop recurrent symptoms. Guidelines for re-initiation of neuroleptic therapy recommend waiting at least two weeks after resolution of symptoms, using lower-potency agents, starting with low doses and slowly titrating to effect, and avoiding lithium in conjunction with neuroleptics. Patients should be instructed to avoid dehydration and should be carefully monitored for any symptoms of recurrent NMS.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroleptic Medications -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: In patients receiving high-risk medications such as neuroleptics, a collaborative monitoring approach involving psychiatrists and internists is crucial for detecting potential signs of serious adverse effects like agranulocytosis, metabolic syndrome, extrapyramidal syndrome, and QT interval prolongation. Emergency clinicians should promptly stabilize patients in cases of neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) or overdose, with supportive care provided under the supervision of critical care specialists. A study assessing the impact of collaborative medication management involving nurses and physicians on patient outcomes in acute psychiatric care for individuals with schizophrenia revealed significant improvements in social functioning among patients in the physician-nurse collaborative group, along with increased medication acceptance. [34] Effective communication and collaboration among clinicians, nurses, and pharmacists within an interprofessional healthcare team is essential for optimizing patient outcomes associated with neuroleptic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Promazine is classified both as an antiemetic and as a dopamine antagonist. As an antiemetic, promazine is used to prevent or treat nausea and vomiting, while its role as a dopamine antagonist means it blocks dopamine receptors, a mechanism that contributes to its anti‑nausea effects and can also be relevant in managing conditions such as psychosis or movement disorders. The overlap between these categories highlights that many dopamine‑blocking agents, including promazine, possess antiemetic properties, which is important for clinicians when selecting appropriate therapy for patients who require control of nausea or benefit from dopamine receptor inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 956
retrieved_docs.content: Mental symptom is a broad clinical concept that includes diverse manifestations such as personality problems, loss of pleasure (anhedonia), and self‑destructive behavior. These mental symptoms are linked to psychotic conditions; specifically, they have a broader relationship with transient organic psychotic states (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة). Transient organic psychotic states are a subtype of organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي), which itself is broadly related to schizophrenic delusion (هوس شَبَقِيّ). The overarching category of psychosis (الذهان) therefore encompasses transient organic psychotic states, situating them within the spectrum of organic psychosis and its association with schizophrenic delusional phenomena. This integrated view highlights how specific mental symptoms connect to distinct forms of organic psychosis and their broader psychotic correlates.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2294

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1353
retrieved_docs.content: Promazine is classified as a dopamine antagonist, meaning it belongs to the group of agents that block dopamine receptors. As a member of this pharmacologic class, promazine exerts its effects by inhibiting dopaminergic neurotransmission, a mechanism commonly utilized in the treatment of psychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia and in managing certain types of nausea. Understanding that promazine functions as a dopamine antagonist helps clinicians anticipate its therapeutic actions as well as potential side effects related to dopamine blockade, such as extrapyramidal symptoms, sedation, and anticholinergic effects. This classification informs appropriate patient care decisions, including drug selection, dosing, and monitoring for adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders serve as a classification for حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة, which are transient organic psychotic conditions. This relationship indicates that brief, organic‑based psychotic episodes are considered within the broader category of anxiety disorders, highlighting an overlap between anxiety symptomatology and short‑lasting psychotic states. Recognizing that حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة fall under anxiety disorders can help clinicians appropriately assess and manage patients who present with transient psychotic features in the context of anxiety‑related pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics are a class of psychotropic medications used to manage conditions such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, and they are distinct from ondansetron, which is a serotonin 5‑HT₃ receptor antagonist employed primarily to prevent nausea and vomiting. A recognized long‑term adverse effect of antipsychotic therapy is the development of tardive movement disorders, referred to in Arabic as “خلل الحركة المتأخر.” This connection highlights the need for careful monitoring of patients receiving antipsychotics for extrapyramidal side effects and differentiating these agents from unrelated drugs like ondansetron.
